questions.questionText: The most common organ injured in blunt abdominal trauma (e.g., steering wheel injury in a motor vehicle collision) is the:
questions.explanation: The speen is highly vascular and friable, making it susceptible to blunt force.

questions.questionText: In penetrating abdominal trauma (e.g., a stab wound to the abdomen), the most commonly injured organ is the:
questions.explanation: The liver is the largest solid organ in the abdomen, frequently extending across the upper quadrants.

questions.questionText: According to Advanced Trauma Life Support (ATLS) protocols, the first priority in the primary survey of a multi-trauma patient is:
questions.optionB: Airway maintenance with cervical spine restriction
questions.explanation: The ABCDE sequence strictly prioritizes establishing a patent airway first while protecting the C-spine.

questions.questionText: A trauma patient presents with profound hypotension, distended neck veins, and absent breath sounds on the right hemithorax. The immediate necessary intervention is:
questions.optionC: Needle decompression in the 2nd intercostal space mid-clavicular line
questions.explanation: This represents a tension pneumothorax causing obstructive shock, requiring immediate decompression before CXR.

questions.questionText: Becks triad (hypotension, distended neck veins, muffled heart sounds) in a chest trauma patient strongly suggests:
questions.explanation: Penetrating injury to the heart fills the pericardium, restricting filling. Treated definitively by pericardial window/thoracotomy.

questions.questionText: The Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) evaluates all of the following parameters EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: GCS (score 3-15) assesses Eye (4), Verbal (5), and Motor (6) responses to gauge consciousness.

questions.questionText: A biconvex (lens-shaped) hyperdensity on a head CT immediately following a head injury with a history of a brief 'lucid interval' is characteristic of an:
questions.explanation: Typically caused by tearing of the middle meningeal artery under the temporal bone.

questions.questionText: A crescent-shaped hyperdensity on a head CT, often seen in the elderly or alcoholics following minor trauma, represents a:
questions.explanation: Resulting from the tearing of bridging veins tracing from the cortex to the dural sinuses.

questions.questionText: Which class of hemorrhagic shock is characterized by a heart rate > 120, a drop in systolic blood pressure, and confusion?
questions.explanation: Class III is the first stage where systolic BP begins to drop, signifying decompensation.

questions.questionText: The definitive test to evaluate for intra-abdominal bleeding in an unstable blunt trauma patient in the emergency department is typically the:
questions.optionA: CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis
questions.optionD: Focused Assessment with Sonography for Trauma (FAST) exam
questions.explanation: The FAST scan rapidly identifies free fluid (blood) in the pericardial, perihepatic, perisplenic, and pelvic spaces.

questions.questionText: In Trauma and Emergency Surgery, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Surgery texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Surgery references for Trauma and Emergency Surgery. Subject: Surgery. Chapter: Trauma and Emergency Surgery.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Trauma and Emergency Surgery scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Surgery. Chapter: Trauma and Emergency Surgery.
